option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
# Core Java Layered System
This document outlines the essential layers and topics required for a comprehensive understanding of Core Java. Mastery of each layer is critical for building robust Java applications, especially in backend development.
---
## 1. Language Foundations - JVM, JRE, JDK architecture - Compilation vs interpretation - Bytecode execution model - Data types (primitive vs reference) - Variables, scope, lifetime - Type casting (implicit, explicit) - Operators (arithmetic, logical, bitwise)
## 2. Control Flow - if / else / switch - loops (for, while, do-while) - break, continue, return - nested flow structures
## 3. Methods - Method definition and invocation - Parameters vs arguments - Pass-by-value (critical concept) - Method overloading - Recursion
## 4. Object-Oriented Programming (Core Layer) - Class and object - Constructors (default, parameterized) - this keyword - Object lifecycle
**Pillars:** - Encapsulation - Inheritance - Polymorphism (compile-time vs runtime) - Abstraction (abstract class vs interface)
## 5. Advanced OOP Concepts - Method overriding - Dynamic method dispatch - super keyword - final keyword (class, method, variable) - Object class methods (toString, equals, hashCode)
## 6. Packages and Access Control - Package creation and usage - import statements - Access modifiers: - public - protected - default - private
## 7. Strings and Immutable Objects - String vs StringBuilder vs StringBuffer - String pool concept - Immutability - Common string operations
## 8. Arrays - Single-dimensional arrays - Multi-dimensional arrays - Memory behavior - Arrays utility class
## 9. Exception Handling - Checked vs unchecked exceptions - try, catch, finally - throw vs throws - Custom exceptions - Exception hierarchy
## 10. Collections Framework (Critical) - List (ArrayList, LinkedList) - Set (HashSet, TreeSet) - Map (HashMap, TreeMap) - Queue, Deque - Iterators - Comparable vs Comparator - Internal working (hashing basics)
## 11. Generics - Type safety - Generic classes and methods - Wildcards (?, extends, super)
## 12. Java I/O (File Handling) - File class - Byte streams vs character streams - Buffered streams - Reading/writing files - Serialization and deserialization
## 13. Multithreading & Concurrency - Thread lifecycle - Creating threads (Thread vs Runnable) - Synchronization - Race conditions - Inter-thread communication - Executor framework (basic)
## 14. Lambda Expressions & Functional Interfaces - Functional interfaces - Lambda syntax - Method references - Stream API (map, filter, reduce)
## 15. Memory Management - Stack vs heap - Garbage collection - Reference types - Memory leaks (logical, not manual)
## 16. Inner Classes - Member inner class - Static nested class - Anonymous class - Local inner class
## 17. Enums and Annotations - Enum basics and use cases - Built-in annotations (@Override, etc.) - Custom annotations (basic understanding)
## 18. Reflection (Awareness Level) - Class metadata - Inspecting methods/fields - Dynamic invocation
## 19. Java Time API - LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime - Formatting and parsing
## 20. Basic Networking (Optional Core Edge) - Sockets (basic idea) - URL and HTTP basics
## 21. JVM Internals (Conceptual) - ClassLoader - Execution engine - JIT compiler - Memory areas (heap, stack, metaspace)
---
### Structural Reality - 1–4 → mandatory base - 5–10 → real programming competence - 11–14 → modern Java capability - 15–21 → system-level understanding
> Skipping order creates shallow knowledge that fails in backend work like Spring.
---
## Progress Tracking
| Layer | Status (Not Started/In Progress/Completed) | Notes | |-------|-------------------------------------------|-------| | 1 | Not Started | | | 2 | Not Started | | | 3 | Not Started | | | 4 | Not Started | | | 5 | Not Started | | | 6 | Not Started | | | 7 | Not Started | | | 8 | Not Started | | | 9 | Not Started | | | 10 | Not Started | | | 11 | Not Started | | | 12 | Not Started | | | 13 | Not Started | | | 14 | Not Started | | | 15 | Not Started | | | 16 | Not Started | | | 17 | Not Started | | | 18 | Not Started | | | 19 | Not Started | | | 20 | Not Started | | | 21 | Not Started | |

option.name: updatedContent
updatedContent
originalContent
updatedContent
updatedContent
updatedContent
option.value:
/* ======================================================== TOPIC: JVM, JRE, JDK ARCHITECTURE ========================================================
Java runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), which provides platform independence by executing compiled bytecode.
-------------------------------------------------------- 1. JDK (Java Development Kit) -------------------------------------------------------- - Complete package for Java development - Contains: JRE + development tools (compiler, debugger, etc.)
-------------------------------------------------------- 2. JRE (Java Runtime Environment) -------------------------------------------------------- - Environment to run Java applications - Contains: JVM + core libraries - No development tools
-------------------------------------------------------- 3. JVM (Java Virtual Machine) -------------------------------------------------------- - Abstract machine that executes Java bytecode - Converts bytecode to machine code (Just-In-Time compilation) - Handles memory management, garbage collection, security, etc.
-------------------------------------------------------- 4. FLOW -------------------------------------------------------- Source Code (.java) → Compiler (javac) → Bytecode (.class) → JVM → Machine Code
-------------------------------------------------------- 5. PLATFORM INDEPENDENCE -------------------------------------------------------- - Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA) - Bytecode is the same for all platforms; JVM implementation is platform-specific
-------------------------------------------------------- 6. KEY POINTS -------------------------------------------------------- - JVM is the heart of Java's portability - JDK is needed for development, JRE for running - JVM manages memory, security, and execution
======================================================== */
package practice;
public class JVMArchitectureNotes { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("JDK = JRE + Development Tools"); System.out.println("JRE = JVM + Core Libraries"); System.out.println("JVM executes bytecode and provides platform independence"); System.out.println("Source → Compiler → Bytecode → JVM → Machine Code"); } }

option.name: updatedContent
originalContent
option.value:
/* ======================================================== TOPIC: OPERATORS IN JAVA ========================================================
Operators are special symbols that perform operations on variables and values.
-------------------------------------------------------- 1. ARITHMETIC OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- + Addition - Subtraction * Multiplication / Division % Modulus (remainder)
-------------------------------------------------------- 2. LOGICAL OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- && Logical AND || Logical OR ! Logical NOT
-------------------------------------------------------- 3. BITWISE OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- & Bitwise AND | Bitwise OR ^ Bitwise XOR ~ Bitwise NOT << Left shift >> Right shift >>> Unsigned right shift
-------------------------------------------------------- 4. OTHER COMMON OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- = Assignment == Equality != Not equal <, >, <=, >= Relational ++ Increment -- Decrement +=, -=, *=, /=, %= Compound assignment
-------------------------------------------------------- 5. EXAMPLES -------------------------------------------------------- int a = 5, b = 2; System.out.println(a + b); // 7 System.out.println(a > b && b > 0); // true System.out.println(a & b); // 0 (bitwise)
-------------------------------------------------------- 6. KEY POINTS -------------------------------------------------------- - Arithmetic: work on numbers - Logical: work on boolean values - Bitwise: work on bits (integers) - Operator precedence affects evaluation order
======================================================== */
package practice;
public class OperatorsNotes { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 5, b = 2; System.out.println("Arithmetic: a + b = " + (a + b)); System.out.println("Logical: (a > b && b > 0) = " + (a > b && b > 0)); System.out.println("Bitwise: a & b = " + (a & b)); System.out.println("Assignment: a = " + a); System.out.println("Equality: a == b? " + (a == b)); System.out.println("Increment: ++a = " + (++a)); System.out.println("Compound assignment: a += b = " + (a += b)); } }

option.name: updatedContent
option.value:
/* ======================================================== TOPIC: OPERATORS IN JAVA ========================================================
Operators are special symbols that perform operations on variables and values.
-------------------------------------------------------- 1. ARITHMETIC OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- + Addition - Subtraction * Multiplication / Division % Modulus (remainder)
-------------------------------------------------------- 2. LOGICAL OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- && Logical AND || Logical OR ! Logical NOT
-------------------------------------------------------- 3. BITWISE OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- & Bitwise AND | Bitwise OR ^ Bitwise XOR ~ Bitwise NOT << Left shift >> Right shift >>> Unsigned right shift
-------------------------------------------------------- 4. OTHER COMMON OPERATORS -------------------------------------------------------- = Assignment == Equality != Not equal <, >, <=, >= Relational ++ Increment -- Decrement +=, -=, *=, /=, %= Compound assignment
-------------------------------------------------------- 5. EXAMPLES -------------------------------------------------------- int a = 5, b = 2; System.out.println(a + b); // 7 System.out.println(a > b && b > 0); // true System.out.println(a & b); // 0 (bitwise)
-------------------------------------------------------- 6. KEY POINTS -------------------------------------------------------- - Arithmetic: work on numbers - Logical: work on boolean values - Bitwise: work on bits (integers) - Operator precedence affects evaluation order
======================================================== */
package practice;
public class OperatorsNotes { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 5, b = 2; System.out.println("Arithmetic: a + b = " + (a + b)); System.out.println("Logical: (a > b && b > 0) = " + (a > b && b > 0)); System.out.println("Bitwise: a & b = " + (a & b)); System.out.println("Assignment: a = " + a); System.out.println("Equality: a == b? " + (a == b)); System.out.println("Increment: ++a = " + (++a)); System.out.println("Compound assignment: a += b = " + (a += b)); } }

option.name: updatedContent
updatedContent
